metadata.title: Guidance on Appropriate Medication for Elderly Patients (general)
metadata.note_on_filename: FILE MISLABELED — filename suggests drug interaction studies but actual content is geriatric polypharmacy guidance
metadata.document_version: HPB/GAD/MSPO Notification No. 0529-1; PSEHB/PSD Notification No. 0529-1
metadata.issuing_authority: Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare (MHLW); translated by PMDA
metadata.pages_reviewed: 1-20 of 39 (full guidance body; pp. 21-39 are appendix drug-reference tables)

rules.rule_text: Elderly patients must be assessed for polypharmacy risk, with particular attention given when 6 or more drugs are used concurrently, as this level of multiple-drug use is associated with a statistically significant increase in adverse drug events.
rules.source_section: Section 1 — Polypharmacy concept; Figure 1
rules.verbatim_quote: some data show that the use of 6 or more drugs was particularly related to an increase in adverse drug events (Figure 1). However, some patients need 6 or more drugs for treatment and other patients may have problems with 3 drugs. In essence, it is how the treatment goes for the patients that counts.
rules.exceptions: Some patients legitimately require 6 or more drugs; clinical judgment and individual patient assessment required. The guidance does not impose a strict numerical cap.
cross_references: Japan Geriatrics Society Guidelines for Medical Treatment and its Safety in the Elderly 2015

rules.rule_text: Prescription review must be conducted at each of the following opportunities: outpatient visit, hospital admission, nursing-facility entry, and when a new acute disease develops or possible adverse drug events are observed.
rules.source_section: Section 3(1) — General rules of prescription review
rules.verbatim_quote: Medication can be reviewed in various treatment environments such as outpatient visit, hospital admission, nursing-facility entry or when a new acute disease develops and possible adverse drug events are observed.

rules.rule_text: Comprehensive Geriatric Assessment (CGA) is required to evaluate cognitive function, activities of daily living (ADL), living environment, drug preference, organ disorders, functional disorders, and ability to self-manage medications in elderly patients.
rules.source_section: Section 3(1) — Comprehensive Geriatric Assessment
rules.verbatim_quote: Assessment of cognitive function, activities of daily living (ADL), living environment, drug preference of each patient, etc., which are the main components of the Comprehensive Geriatric Assessment (CGA), will contribute to understanding of their organ disorders, functional disorders, and the ability to manage their own medications.

rules.rule_text: The prescriber must grasp all departments and medical institutions the elderly patient is visiting and check all prescribed drugs including guidance-mandatory drugs, over-the-counter drugs, and supplements, together with medication adherence status.
rules.source_section: Section 3(1) — Comprehensive Geriatric Assessment
rules.verbatim_quote: It is important to grasp all the departments/medical institutions the patient is visiting in this process and to check all prescribed drugs (including guidance-mandatory drugs/over-the-counter drugs, (hereinafter referred to as 'over-the-counter drugs'), supplements, etc.) and medication adherence.

rules.rule_text: For drugs mainly excreted by the kidneys, elderly patients must be monitored for decreased renal function with aging as well as adverse drug events; careful administration including dose reduction and prolonged dosing interval must be considered.
rules.source_section: Section 3(1) — Monitoring physiological functions, such as renal function
rules.verbatim_quote: For drugs that are mainly excreted by the kidneys, the patients should be monitored for decreased physiological functions such as renal function with aging as well as adverse drug events, etc. Careful administration should be considered, like dose reduction, and prolonged dosing interval.

rules.rule_text: In principle, drugs prescribed to elderly patients must be started at a lower dose (e.g., 1/2 to 1/3 of the usual dose) and titrated upward while monitoring effectiveness and adverse events.
rules.source_section: Section 5(1) — The initial dose and dose-adjustment method tailored to drug characteristics
rules.verbatim_quote: Since the elderly tend to experience an increase in the maximum blood concentration of a drug and its protracted elimination from the body, it is necessary to reduce doses and administer them at longer intervals. Therefore, in principle, the drug is started at a lower dose (e.g. 1/2 to 1/3 of usual dose), and it should be titrated up while its effectiveness and adverse events are monitored.
rules.exceptions: Dose should be carefully set especially for high-risk drugs (antidiabetic drugs, digitalis drugs, antiepileptic drugs, etc.).

rules.rule_text: Prescription review for each drug must apply a structured decision process (Fig. 4-2 criteria): (1) Is the therapy within recommended scope? (2) Is the therapy effective? (3) Are dose reduction or discontinuation possible? (4) Is there a substitute drug? Efficacy and side effects in patient treatment history must be verified.
rules.source_section: Section 3(4) — Flow of general ways of thinking; Figure 4-2
rules.verbatim_quote: Therapy optimization (determination whether to discontinue, adjust, or continue) Determine based on the following criteria: Is the therapy within the recommended scope of use? Is the therapy effective? Are dose reduction or discontinuation possible? Is there a substitute drug? Verify efficacy and side effects in the patient's treatment history. Reconsider which drugs are most effective.

rules.rule_text: When a drug is discontinued in elderly patients, it must proceed step by step, taking into account an increased risk of sudden aggravation of diseases and adverse events.
rules.source_section: Section 3(1) — Prescription priority and dose reduction/drug discontinuation
rules.verbatim_quote: When a drug is discontinued, it should proceed step by step, taking into account an increased risk of sudden aggravation of diseases and adverse events.
rules.exceptions: Acute phase — when adverse drug events are suspected, as many drugs as possible should be discontinued and clinical course monitored.

rules.rule_text: At each medical institution and pharmacy, overlapping prescriptions among drugs of the same type or with the same therapeutic effects must be checked for each therapeutic category to prevent adverse drug events.
rules.source_section: Section 5(3)1 — Check of overlapping prescriptions
rules.verbatim_quote: To prevent the risk of adverse drug events that may result in significant health damage, it is necessary to check at each medical institution and pharmacy for overlapping of prescriptions among drugs of the same type or with the same therapeutic effect for each therapeutic category.

rules.rule_text: New drugs should not easily be prescribed to elderly patients because the elderly are sometimes excluded from clinical trial subject groups; the prescriber must give due consideration to risk-benefit balance.
rules.source_section: Section 3(3) — Specialist perspective
rules.verbatim_quote: the elderly are sometimes excluded from the subject groups in clinical trials for new drugs. Therefore, new drugs should not easily be prescribed to the elderly.
rules.exceptions: When a patient is under a serious disease condition and treatment is urgently needed, medication aimed at better outcomes may be selected even if accompanied by higher risk.

rules.rule_text: Discontinuation of polypharmacy requires cooperation among healthcare professionals and education to patients; it is related to ensuring a complete understanding of the whole prescription status across all treating institutions.
rules.source_section: Section 2 — Status of multiple-drug use
rules.verbatim_quote: Discontinuation of polypharmacy requires cooperation among healthcare professionals and education to patients because it is related to a failure to grasp the whole prescription status and overlapped prescriptions due to an increase in comorbidities and medical consultation at multiple departments/medical institutions.

rules.rule_text: Non-medication therapy must be considered and applied prior to medication for lifestyle-related diseases and BPSD (behavioral and psychological symptoms of dementia). Physical constraint without proper use of drugs is not recommended when BPSD occurs.
rules.source_section: Section 3(2) — Importance of non-medication therapy
rules.verbatim_quote: non-medication therapy which improves lifestyle may be useful in disease treatment for the elderly. In such a case, it should be provided prior to medication... The drugs used for BPSD may cause adverse drug events that affect ADL, such as extrapyramidal disorder and over-sedation. Therefore, non-medication therapy is recommended first... Physical constraint without proper use of drugs is not recommended when BPSD occurs.
rules.exceptions: When disease requires urgent treatment, medication may be selected despite higher risk.

rules.rule_text: At transitions in care environment (hospital discharge/transfer, nursing-care facility admission, home-based medical care, new treatment by primary care physician), prescriptions must be reviewed comprehensively, assuming continued management in the new environment.
rules.source_section: Section 5(5) — Timing of prescription review; upon changes in care environment
rules.verbatim_quote: admission/relocation to a nursing-care facility, introduction to home-based medical care, and starting new treatment by a primary care physician, prescriptions must be reviewed assuming a continued management of the patient in the new environment (Figure 5).

rules.rule_text: Prescribers must grasp how elderly patients use over-the-counter drugs, Chinese medicines, health foods, and supplements — including frequency and dose — to ensure safety and prevent adverse drug interactions.
rules.source_section: Section 5(4)2 — Over-the-counter drugs, health food (including supplements)
rules.verbatim_quote: it is important to grasp how patients use such drugs or supplements (frequency and dose) to ensure safety by urging the patient, their family, nursing staff to be aware of the importance.

rules.rule_text: When a combination of drugs that may cause drug-induced actions is prescribed, the prescriber must monitor efficacy and adverse effects and correct the prescription by discontinuation, dose reduction, or switching to alternative drugs, taking into account prescription background, patient background, severity of potential interactions, and information on alternative drugs.
rules.source_section: Section 5(3)2 — Prevention and management of drug interactions
rules.verbatim_quote: When a combination of drugs that may cause drug-induced actions is prescribed, it is important to correct the prescription by monitoring the efficacy and adverse effect, discontinuation, dose reduction, or switching to alternative drugs, taking into account prescription backgrounds, patient backgrounds, seriousness of actions that can be caused by drug interactions, and information on alternative drugs.
cross_references: Appendix Table 4 — CYP substrates, inhibitors, inducers

rules.rule_text: Prescribers must consider the possibility that newly appearing symptoms or signs in elderly patients are adverse drug events (ADEs) from existing medications before issuing new prescriptions, to prevent the prescription cascade.
rules.source_section: Section 2(2) — Polypharmacy formation; Section 4
rules.verbatim_quote: when every new disease is treated with medication, the practice may fall into the 'prescription cascade,' a vicious cycle where new prescriptions are issued to address ADEs associated with previously prescribed drugs (Figure 3, Example 2)... If there are any symptoms/findings including geriatric syndromes for which a relation with drugs is suspected, prescription should be checked and discontinuation/dose reduction should be considered first.

rules.rule_text: Specialists treating elderly patients must understand the need for collaboration with other specialists, primary care physicians, and other healthcare professions for appropriate medication management.
rules.source_section: Section 3(3) — Specialist perspective
rules.verbatim_quote: Specialists in a certain area are not the specialists in other areas. It is necessary to understand the need in collaboration with other specialists, primary care physicians, and other types of professions for more appropriate medication.

rules.rule_text: Doses for high-risk drugs (antidiabetic drugs, digitalis drugs, antiepileptic drugs) in elderly patients must be set with especially careful consideration of renal function and individualized patient characteristics.
rules.source_section: Section 5(1) — Initial dose and dose-adjustment method
rules.verbatim_quote: The dose should be carefully set especially for so-called high-risk drugs (antidiabetic drugs, digitalis drugs, antiepileptic drugs, etc.). Typical drugs that are excreted by the kidneys are as shown in Appendix Table 3, and their doses and appropriateness of coadministered drugs should be determined taking into account the renal function of the patient as specified in the attached document of appendix.
cross_references: Appendix Table 3 — renally excreted drugs and dosing

rules.rule_text: Approximately 250 adverse drug events related to over-the-counter drugs are annually reported to MHLW; concomitant use of health food with prescription drugs can greatly affect treatment effectiveness and must be assessed and documented.
rules.source_section: Section 5(4)2 — Adverse drug events related to OTC drugs and health food
rules.verbatim_quote: Concomitant use of health food with drugs can greatly affect treatment effectiveness. Approximately 250 adverse drug events related to over-the-counter drugs, etc. are annually reported to MHLW and other related organizations.

rules.rule_text: Each prescribed drug in an elderly patient must be assessed for: (a) whether evidence of prophylaxis is valid for the elderly, (b) whether symptomatic treatment is effective or other non-medication measures are available, and (c) whether the drug aligns with treatment priorities.
rules.source_section: Section 3(1) — Points to review the appropriateness of each drug
rules.verbatim_quote: Points to review the appropriateness of each drug: Whether the evidence of prophylaxis drugs is valid for the elderly; Whether symptomatic treatment is effective, or whether there are any other measures than medications; Whether it is the treatment policy according to treatment priority, etc.
